info.title: Issuetrak Api
info.description:
# Getting Started The purpose of this page is to document the usage and operations on Issuetrak's API v2. It should be assumed that all references to "API" on this page are referring to API v2 unless otherwise noted. If you encounter problems that you are unable to resolve, please reach out to [Issuetrak Support](https://support.issuetrak.com/). #### Table of Contents * [Managing the API](#sec-managing) * [Authentication and Authorization](#sec-authentication-and-authorization) * [How Authorization Works](#sec-authorization) * [Authentication](#sec-authentication) * [API Tokens](#sec-tokens) * [Using the API](#sec-usage-guide) * [IID Explained](#sec-iid) * [Create, Update, and Read-Only Schemas](#sec-operational-schemas) * [PATCH Endpoints](#sec-patch) * [Path](#sec-patch-path) * [Operations](#sec-patch-operations) * [Managing Memberships](#sec-memberships) * [Retrieving Lists](#sec-lists) * [Paging and Sorting](#sec-page-and-sort) * [Filtering](#sec-filtering) * [Filter vs. Criteria](#sec-criteria) * [Anatomy of Criteria](#sec-criteria-anatomy) * [User Defined Field Handling](#sec-udf) * [UDF Values vs. Definitions](#sec-udf-value-vs-def) * [UDF Searching](#sec-udf-searching) * [Troubleshooting](#sec-troubleshooting) * [Troubleshooting a 400 Status Code](#sec-troubelshoot-400) * [Troubleshooting a 401 Status Code](#sec-troubelshoot-401) * [Troubleshooting a 403 Status Code](#sec-troubelshoot-403) * [Troubleshooting a 404 Status Code](#sec-troubelshoot-404) * [Troubleshooting a 429 Status Code](#sec-troubelshoot-429) * [Troubleshooting Unexpected null Values on Responses](#sec-troubelshoot-null') # Managing the API This page is primarily focused on interacting with the API. For information regarding the management of the API, including logging, rate-limiting, and system requirements, please visit our [Help Center](https://helpcenter.issuetrak.com/home/2463-api-v2-overview). # Authentication and Authorization **Highlights:** * API tokens are used for authentication to the API * API tokens are created for Issuetrak Agent accounts * API operations are restricted according to the permissions of the account the token was issued to ## How Authorization Works The API is accessible by any user with the permission "Can authenticate with the API v2". Any user with this permission is authorized to access the API, but it does not grant them full access. Operations performed against the API respect user permissions in the same way the website does. However. there is one caveat. The website requires a user to have permission to log in, but the API only requires users to have permission to authenticate with the API. ## Authentication Authenticating against the Issuetrak API is done with API tokens. ### API Tokens API tokens are used for authenticating against the API. Tokens can be managed from the API tokens page, accessible by clicking the Settings lightbox, selecting API v2 underneath Integrations, and then clicking Tokens on the right context menu. API tokens can be used to authenticate anyone with access to the API. System administrators can create tokens for any user with the appropriate permissions, but other users are restricted to only creating tokens for themselves. #### Creating API Tokens When creating a token, you'll be asked for a name, expiration date, and user (only system administrators will be asked for the user field). The Token Name is required, but it is only used as the visible identifier on the list. It is suggested that you use a meaningful name that corresponds with the use case of the token. For example, if you are writing an integration between Issuetrak and your chat client, you may want to name the token "Chat Client". This way, it will be easy to remember what that token is used for. The Expiration Date is an optional (but recommended) field. If provided, the token will stop working after the date specified. This is a security measure taken in order for you to enforce your own key rotation policies. If this field is left empty, the token will remain usable until it is manually revoked. The User field, which is only visible to system administrators, allows you to specify who the token represents in Issuetrak. For example: If we have a dedicated account with the user ID "IntegrationUser", a system administrator can select "IntegrationUser" as the user when creating a token, and usage of that token will tell the API to treat requests as if they were performed by "IntegrationUser" as opposed to the system administrator who created the token. If the user field is left blank, then that token will be created to represent the user who created the token. Once you have filled in the form for creating a token and click on Create Token, the lightbox on the page will be updated to present the token. At this point, it is very important that you copy that token and save it somewhere secure because this is the only point in time where the value of the token will be given. If you make a mistake and forget to save the token value, that is not a problem. The token will appear on the API Token table, and from there you can simply click the "Revoke" button. This button effectively deletes the token, rendering it unusable. Simply delete the token whose value you did not copy, create another new one, and copy the new API Token before closing the lightbox. #### Using API Tokens The API token value presented upon creation is the value you will use for authenticating against the API. Here is an example of using the API token when making a request: `curl -H "X-Api-Key: {the token you retrieved goes here}"`. Omit the curly braces shown in the example above when you submit an API key authorization. # Using the API ## IID Explained **Highlights:** * IID stands for "Internal Identifier" * IID is used for referencing objects throughout the API * IID is a serverside generated, read-only, and unique identifier that represents a pointer to an item in the application * IID is used in the route when attempting to access or perform an operation against a specific entity The IID is an acronym that stands for "Internal Identifier". It is a field generated by the application to represent a unique entity in the application. It is not of a specific type. For example: Users have an IID which is of type integer, while Issue Hub Views have an IID that is a GUID string. The purpose of the IID is to provide a consistent interface for referencing entities. For example: Let's say we want to get information about an organization. We know that the IID of the organization is 4. We can retrieve that entity by calling `GET /Organizations/4`. Another usage of IID is cross-referencing. When we create a user, there are multiple IID fields in the `CreateUser` schema: `departmentIid`, `primaryOrganizationIid`, `locationIid`, and `issueHubViewIid` (There's also the IIDs referenced by the `udfs` field, but we'll discuss that more below). Those fields are used as pointers to other objects. If you want to specify a department for a user, you would use the department's IID as the value when updating or creating that user. This structure allows for easy chaining. For instance, let's say we want to get details about the primary organization of a user, but all we know is that the user's IID is 3. If we pull information about the user by sending the request `GET /Users/3`, and we receive an object with `primaryOrganizationIid: 7`, we can make a call to `GET /Organizations/7` to obtain more information about the referenced organization. > **In many cases, the IID will have the same value as the ID, but always be sure to use the IID when you're attempting to access or reference an entity when using this API.** ## Create, Update and Read-Only Schemas **Highlights:** * Different operations use different schemas in reference to the same resource * All operations on a resource return the same schema, regardless of the schema they accept * The returned schema extends the other schemas and can be used as the body for other operations on the resource Objects in this API will typically have three schema representations which are indicated by their prefix. These representations typically use either no prefix or the prefixes `Create` or `Update`. For instance, there are three schemas associated with organizations: `CreateOrganization`, `UpdateOrganization`, and `Organization`. The read-only schema representation (in this case, `Organization`) is the only representation that will be returned from the API and it represents the true state of the object in Issuetrak. The other two are used exclusively to represent the shape of data accepted in the body of `POST` and `PUT` requests. To continue with the specific example using organizations, `POST /Organizations` accepts a `CreateOrganization` in the body, while `PUT /Organizations/{iid}` accepts an `UpdateOrganization` in the body. Both of those operations return an `Organization` schema. The purpose of these different shapes is to separate the concerns of different operations. Each schema only contains values that matter for the operation to which they are connected. Continuing with the organization example, only the `Organization` schema contains a field for `modifiedDate` because this field can not be altered directly. Its value is determined internally by Issuetrak. While fields like `modifiedDate` are not on the `UpdateOrganization` model, including them will not cause an error because they are simply ignored. This can potentially simplify your workflow. For example: Let's say that you want to update an organization's email. You would start by retrieving that organization (either using `GET /Organizations`, `POST /Organizations/Search`, or if you know the organizations IID, `GET /Organizations/{iid}`). This will give you the `Organization` schema representation of that organization. In your code, you could simply change the `email` field of the object and use that same object as your body for `PUT /Organizations/{iid}` and to update that field. There is no need to update fields like `modfiedDate`, or remove fields like `iid` to force the object to match the `UpdateOrganization` schema. ## PATCH Endpoints **Highlights:** - Can be used to perform a partial update to an object - The path property is in the format `(/[a-z0-9]+)+` - Available operations are `Replace` and `Remove` - The body of a PATCH request is an array of instructions that you want to perform on the object With PATCH operations, you can change specific details of an object without needing to get the entire object first. This makes updates quicker and more efficient. The body of a PATCH request is an array of instructions that you want to perform on the object. Each operation consists of a path, an operation, and a value. Here is an example of a PATCH request that changes the name of an organization, changes the contact user, sets the organization to internal, and removes a date field from the organization's UDFs: ```json [ { "op": "Replace", "path": "/Name", "value": "Issuetrak" } , { "op": "Replace", "path": "/contactUserIid", "value": 38 } , { "op": "Replace", "path": "/IsInternal", "value": true } , { "op": "Remove", "path": "/UDFs/dateFields/6"} ] ``` ### Path The "path" is like an address that points to the exact part of the object you want to change. It’s made up of several parts, each starting with a /, and each part corresponds to a property of the object. For example, if you want to change the "Name" property of an organization, the path would be "/Name". If you want to change the "City" property of the "Primary Location" of an organization, the path would be "/PrimaryLocation/City". ### Operations There are two operations that can be performed on an object with a PATCH request: - Remove: This operation clears out a property. However, you can't remove required fields. For example, you can't remove the name of an organization. - Replace: This operation sets or changes the value of a property. ## Managing Memberships **Highlights:** * Memberships are managed as sub-resources * All membership operations are done as bulk operations Some objects in Issuetrak have relationships through memberships which can be managed through the API. For instance, a user can be a member of groups and organizations. These relationships can be managed as sub-resources. For instance, let’s say we have a user with an IID of 4. That user’s group memberships could be managed through the `/Users/4/Memberships/Groups` endpoint. From there we can add and remove memberships using the `POST` and `DELETE` methods respectively. Membership endpoints accept lists as their parameters. For instance, when adding organization memberships to a user, we can specify an array of organization membership objects in the body, which will all be added to the user. The same holds true for deleting organization memberships. All membership operations are done as bulk operations. With more complex relationships that have secondary properties (such as organization memberships), we also have an update method. Calling `PUT /Users/4/Memberships/Organizations` will allow us to update the flags on our organization memberships (such as `canViewUsers`). These `PUT` methods only allow us to update memberships that are already present on the object; we can not add or remove memberships using the `PUT` method. ## Retrieving lists **Highlights:** * Sorting and Paging is supported on both `GET /{resource}` and `POST /{resource}/Search` * Filtering is only supported by `POST /{resource}/Search` endpoints * Filters consist of criteria objects and the returned values will only be ones that meet all of the specified criteria For most resources, there are two ways to retrieve lists. In this instance, resource refers to a collection of entities. For example, let's use the example of users. We can retrieve lists of users by either using the method `GET /Users` or `POST /Users/Search`. Each of these endpoints will return paged lists of users. ### Paging and Sorting Both `GET` and `POST` methods allow for paging and sorting where applicable. This means that you can specify things like page size, page number, sort field, and sort direction on either of these requests using the appropriate method of passing data (query string parameters for `GET /Users` and the body for `POST /Users/Search`). *There is no way to filter using the `GET` method.* > **Not all** `GET /{resource}/*` methods support paging and sorting. For example `GET /Users/Udfs` returns a single response and does not accept any parameters. ### Filtering Filtering through the API is done via `POST /{resource}/Search` endpoints. These endpoints accept a JSON body that is used for paging, sorting, and filtering. The schema they accept is a filter that corresponds to the type of entity they return. For example, The `POST /Users/Search` endpoint returns a paged list of users and accepts a `UserFilter`. #### Filter vs. Criteria The endpoint `POST /Users/Search` accepts a `UserFilter` which consists of multiple criteria specifications. Other examples of filters would be things like `GroupFilter`, `OrganizationFilter`, and `LocationFilter`. Examples of criteria specifications would be things like `StringCriteria`, `IntCriteria`, and `DateOnlyCriteria`. To explain how this relationship works, let's say we wanted to find all of the users whose first name is either "John" or "Diane" and whose last name is "Young". We could call the method `POST /Users/Search` with a body that looks like this: 
{
  "firstName": {
    "type": "matchAny"
    , "values": ["Diane", "John"]
  },
  "lastName": {
    "type": "matchAny"
    , "values": ["Young"]
  }
}
Let's break down what is going on. If we look at the definition of the endpoint `POST /Users/Search`, we see that it takes a `UserFilter` and that the `UserFilter` is comprised of many fields. All of these fields are optional, but in the case of the above example, we see that we're specifying `firstName` and `lastName`. These fields correspond to fields on the `User` schema, which the endpoint will return. If we look more closely, we will see that everything on the `UserFilter` (other than information regarding paging and sorting) corresponds to a field on the `User` schema, but instead of being raw data values (`string`, `boolean`, etc.), they are defined as a corresponding "criteria". For example `firstName` on the `User` schema is of type `string`, but `firstName` on the `UserFilter` is of type `StringCriteria`. This enables us to write complex filter operations that fit a wider variety of use-cases. When calling a search endpoint with a filter, the returned values will be filtered to only include results that meet the entire criteria given. #### Anatomy of Criteria Nearly all of the `*Criteria` schemas have two parts; type and values. The `type` field specifies a condition. In the case of the criteria we defined in our example (for `firstName` and `lastName`) we used a `StringCriteria` and we defined it with `"type": "matchAny"`. This means that results will only be returned if the value in that field on the existing users matches any of the values specified in the `values` section of that criteria. There are many different types available, and the available type options are likely to grow in the future. The available type options are also unique to the criteria type. For instance, `IntCriteria` has a type option for `MatchAny`, but not one for `Contains`. The `values` field indicates the values which you want to compare the field against using the `type` value. For example: In the case of `matchAny`, we are saying that the value in the field "Must match one of the values specified in the `values` field". Some operations require there to be only one value in the `values` field, such as in a scenarion in which we define a `StringCriteria` with `"type": "contains"`. Some operations do not want any values at all, such as when you use `BooleanCriteria` and `"type": "isFalse"`. The available types, their behaviors, and expectations about the contents of `values` can be found on the documentation of the relevant `*Criteria` schema. ## User Defined Field Handling **Highlights:** * User defined fields (UDFs) are treated as distinct resources with their own IIDs * UDF values on objects (Users, Organizations, etc.) reference the IID of their corresponding UDF * List UDF options also have their own IIDs which are used as the value on objects that reference them For the purposes of brevity and creating an association between this documentation and the objects you'll use when interacting with this API, User Defined Fields will be referred to as UDFs going forward. In the product, you typically interact with UDFs as fields that have a label that corresponds with the label defined on the UDF settings page. For instance, if you have defined a list UDF for users in the application, and have given that UDF the label "Homeroom teacher", then you would see the field on the user page as "Homeroom teacher". This process of custom labeling does not translate well to an API, as dynamic field keys are unsafe and can cause inconsistent and difficult to maintain integrations. This API solves this problem by treating UDFs as themselves entities. ### UDF Values vs. Definitions When we retrieve a user through the API, we receive a member property titled `udfs`. This property contains an object with the following fields: `textFields`, `dateFields`, and `listFields`. Each of these fields contains an array of objects with two fields: `iid` and `value`. `iid` represents the IID of the corresponding UDF definition, while value represents the value that is in that field on the user object. While these fields represent the UDF values for the specified user, we can also retrieve the definitions of the UDFs from a separate endpoint. `GET /Users/Udfs` will return an object that looks very similar to the `udfs` field we saw when retrieving the user. This is an object with three fields, `textFields`, `dateFields`, and `listFields`. The difference here is that instead of these fields containing an array of UDF values, they instead contain an array of UDF definitions. Instead of a value, we get the actual name associated with the UDF. That name corresponds with the label you would see when editing or adding a user through the website. In the case of `listFields`, we also get a separate field titled `options`. This field holds the options defined for that UDF, which correspond to the dropdown options you would see when editing a list UDF on a user through the application. You will notice when looking at the response that the objects listed in the `options` field also have an `iid` field. This is because when we interact with the list fields on a user through the API, we use the `iid` of the option as the value instead of the text you would see through the website. This ensures the value of the UDF on the user remains persistent, regardless of changes you might make through the UI. Let's imagine an example where you are using a list field for users with the label "Homeroom teacher". Then let's say one of the teachers goes through a life event that causes them to change their name. Rather than having to update all of the user's UDF values to reflect this change, we simply need to update the UDF option definition, and since all of the relationships are linked by the IID, there is no change needed at the level of the users. ### UDF Searching When searching for entities with UDFs, the relevant filter object will contain a `udfs` field which appears very similar to the object when retrieving the definitions or when performing read/write operations against an entity with UDFs. The difference is that the fields contain arrays of UDF filters. UDF filters consist of two fields. The field `iid` represents the IID of the UDF you wish to search in relation to, and `filter` defines the filter for that data type. You can read more about the data type filter in the section "List versus Search". For an example of how this works, let's imagine a situation where you have a text UDF with the label "Employee Badge Number" and we're trying to retrieve a user with the badge number "XYZ-1234". We can call the `GET /Users/Udfs` endpoint to retrieve the definition of UDFs and we receive: 
{
  "textFields": [
    {
      "iid": 7,
      "name": "Employee Badge Number"
    }
  ],
  "dateFields": [],
  "listFields": [],
}
We can then call the `POST /Users/Search` endpoint with this filter: 
{
  "udfs": {
    "textFields": [
      {
        "iid": 7,
        "filter": {
          "type": "matchAny",
          "values": ["XYZ-1234"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
Notice that this filter defined the `udfs` field with the `textFields` array. We defined this filter with the `iid` set to `7` because that is the `iid` of the "Employee Badge Number" field. We then set the `filter` as a `StringFilter` with the `type` as `matchAny` and the `values` as `["XYZ-1234"]`. # Troubleshooting This section deals with common error codes and problems with using the API. If you encounter problems that you are unable to resolve, please reach out to [Issuetrak Support](https://support.issuetrak.com/). ## Troubleshooting a 400 Status Code There are two scenarios where you may receive a `400` error, and they will result in differently formatted responses. ### Scenario 1 In the first case, you will receive an error with properties such as `type`, `status`, and `title`. This is most likely a formatting error and there are a couple of reasons you might be seeing it. If you look at the errors field, you should receive helpful information for your specific case. Check that your request body was formatted properly. Ensure that the body is proper JSON, and adheres to the schema specified by the endpoint. If you are using a schema with an enumerated value, ensure that you are providing a value that is specified in the documentation as a valid option for that enumerated field. For instance, on any filter with criteria, the type field is an enumerated value. ### Scenario 2 The second scenario comes when your data is formatted correctly, but the data provided was not valid for whatever reason. In this case, you should get an `information` field which contains an array of debugging messages on the returned object. This should provide troubleshooting information for your request. ## Troubleshooting a 401 Status Code This is likely caused by a misconfiguration of the `X-Api-key` header. Ensure that the header exists and is being sent with your request. Then ensure that the value of the header is the token value you wanted to send. For security reasons; there is no way to retrieve a token value after creation. If you are unsure about the validity of your token; try creating a new token and using the value of the new token as the value of the header in your request. ## Troubleshooting a 403 Status Code A `403` is returned when the acting user does not have the permissions necessary to perform a request. For instance, if a user without access to administrative functions attempts to list all departments, they will receive a `403` status code because they do not have permission to access departments. With operations like `GET /Locations`, you should think of the endpoint as analogous to the *Locations List* screen in Issuetrak. If a user can access the *Locations List* screen in Issuetrak, then they will be able to access the list methods for that resource in the API. A user who can only see locations through a dropdown on a page such as *Submit Issue* may not have permission to retrieve a list of locations through the API. ## Troubleshooting a 404 Status Code `404` errors are returned when the resource does not exist. For instance, let's say we attempt to call `GET /users/23/memberships/groups`. If there is no user with the IID 23, then we will receive a `404`, because the resource `/users/23` does not exist. Similarly, if we attempt to access an endpoint that does not exist (i.e. `GET /nonexistent`) then that will also return a `404`. `404` responses are also returned when the user making the request should not be able to see the resource. For instance, a system administrator may be able to access the endpoint `GET /users/24` and return a user. A user with permission to manage users in their organization may receive a `404` for the same exact request if that user should not be able to access that user. You might expect that user to receive a `403` error code indicating that they are unauthorized to access user `24`. For security reasons, we do not want to tell users that something exists if they don't have access to it. If we returned a `403` in the previous scenario, we'd be effectively telling the user "there is a user here that you can't see". If the user can't see a resource we don't want to indirectly indicate that the resource exists by telling them that they are unauthorized to see it. ## Troubleshooting a 429 Status Code `429` error codes happen when you have exceeded the rate at which API requests are allowed to be made. To troubleshoot this, you must either reduce the frequency of requests you are making, or contact your site administrator and request an elevation of the rate limits. More information regarding rate limits can be found in our [Help Center](https://helpcenter.issuetrak.com/home/2463-api-v2-overview#ratelimits). ## Troubleshooting Unexpected null Values on Responses There are a couple of reasons why you may see `null` on a field on an object. One example may be if the relevant feature is disabled. For instance, if a user has a `locationIid` set to `6`, but we then disable locations within the Issuetrak application, all users will return `locationIid` as `null`. The location stored on the user will still technically be present, but it can not be accessed or changed while locations are disabled. Another example is when a reference has been deleted. For example; we might expect every entity to have a valid `createdByUserIid`; but if the user that created something has been deleted, then that thing will have a `createdByUserIid` equal to `null`.

DateOnlyCriteria.description: Criteria objects are used to specify conditions when filtering a resource.  [Click here for more information.](#sec-criteria-anatomy)

DateOnlyMatchType.description:
| Type | Requirements | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| MatchAny | `values` contains at least one item | Returns objects where the value matches one of the items in `values` |
| IsEmpty | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the value is null or empty |
| HasValue | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the value is neither null nor empty |
| CustomRange | `values` contains exactly 2 items | Returns objects where the field value falls between the two specified dates (inclusive) |
| Today | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value is today's date |
| Yesterday | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value is yesterday's date |
| Tomorrow | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value is tomorrow's date |
| ThisWeek | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the current week (Sunday to Saturday, inclusive) |
| LastWeek | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the previous week (Sunday to Saturday, inclusive) |
| NextWeek | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the next week (Sunday to Saturday, inclusive) |
| ThisMonth | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the current month (1st to last day, inclusive) |
| LastMonth | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the previous month (1st to last day, inclusive) |
| NextMonth | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the next month (1st to last day, inclusive) |
| ThisYear | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the current year (January 1st to December 31st, inclusive) |
| LastYear | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the previous year (January 1st to December 31st, inclusive) |
| NextYear | `values` is empty | Returns objects where the field value falls within the next year (January 1st to December 31st, inclusive) |

PatchOperation.description:
There are three supported operations: Add, Replace, and Remove.  
- Replace: Replace an item in a collection such as UDFS, or update an existing property
- Remove: Remove an item from a collection such as UDFS, or clear out a property

path.description:
The path to the property or item to be modified. The path is a sequence of one or more tokens, each prefixed by a '/'. Collections of items are indexed with the item IID.  
Examples:
- `/udf/TextFields/4`: addresses the text UDF with IID 4 from the entity
- `/name`: addresses the name of the entity
